overview.text: ABC News is the news and current affairs division of the Australian Broadcasting Corporation (ABC), Australia's national public broadcaster. It is responsible for all news-gathering and coverage across the ABC's television, radio, and online platforms.

ABC News operates nationally across radio, television, and digital media, with production studios in capital cities around the country. Its main operations are based in New South Wales, with headquarters at Ultimo in Sydney and additional production facilities at Parramatta.
At a Glance

items: Parent organisation: the Australian Broadcasting Corporation (ABC)
Established on 1 June 1947
Television: the ABC News Channel, a 24-hour news channel
Radio: ABC NewsRadio, plus programs such as AM, The World Today, and PM
Online: the ABC News website at abc.net.au/news, along with ABC iview and the ABC Listen app
In 2018, ABC online news and current affairs reached about 4.8 million users in Australia each month
Headquarters at Ultimo, New South Wales, with additional Sydney production at Parramatta

overview.text: The Australian Capital Territory (ACT) Government is the self-governing territory administration for the ACT, centred on Canberra. Unlike the Australian states, the ACT has no separate tier of local government: functions normally handled by local councils are delivered directly by the territory government, so the ACT Government combines state and local roles in a single administration.

Although this is a NSW-focused public-safety monitoring site, the ACT is relevant because it is entirely surrounded by New South Wales. The ACT Emergency Services Agency operates right on the NSW border, and its services routinely work alongside neighbouring NSW agencies, making cross-border coordination and mutual aid a normal part of emergency response in the region.
At a Glance

items: Self-government commenced on 4 March 1989.
Governed by the unicameral Australian Capital Territory Legislative Assembly, which directly elects the Chief Minister from among its members.
Combines state and local government functions, with no separate system of local government.
Executive power rests with the Chief Minister and ministers, who form the Cabinet.
Emergency response is coordinated through the ACT Emergency Services Agency.
The agency oversees ambulance, fire and rescue, rural fire service and State Emergency Service operations.
Services are administered through directorates and accessed by the public via Access Canberra.

overview.text: Ausgrid owns and operates the electricity distribution network across Sydney, the Central Coast and the Hunter Region. It carries power from the high-voltage transmission grid to about 1.7 million homes and businesses.
Coverage

items: Around 22,000 km² of NSW from Waterfall to Upper Hunter.
Roughly 30,000 km of overhead and underground network.
24/7 control room dispatches outage and emergency response crews.

overview.text: The Australian Federal Police (AFP) is the principal federal law enforcement agency of the Australian Government, responsible for enforcing Commonwealth criminal law and protecting national security. Its remit covers transnational and organised crime, terrorism and violent extremism, cybercrime, child exploitation, drug importation, human trafficking and money laundering, alongside aviation security and close personal protection for dignitaries.

In New South Wales the AFP's role is federal rather than general policing, which remains the responsibility of the NSW Police Force. The AFP investigates Commonwealth offences committed in the state, secures major airports including Sydney Airport, and leads counter-terrorism and serious organised crime work in partnership with state agencies.
At a Glance

items: Established 19 October 1979 under the Australian Federal Police Act 1979
Employs approximately 8,082 staff (2024)
Operates within the Home Affairs portfolio; the responsible minister is the Minister for Home Affairs
Commissioner Krissy Barrett took office on 4 October 2025
Provides aviation security at nine major airports
Maintains around 85 international liaison officers in about 40 countries
Delivers community policing in the Australian Capital Territory and external territories
Headquartered at the Edmund Barton Building, Canberra

overview.text: Formation

items: Created by merging the Commonwealth Police, ACT Police and the Commonwealth Narcotics Bureau
Its creation followed the 13 February 1978 Sydney Hilton Hotel bombing and a review by Sir Robert Mark
Sir Colin Woods served as the first Commissioner

overview.text: The Australian Rail Track Corporation is a Commonwealth-owned company that manages and maintains most of the interstate standard-gauge rail network. In NSW it operates the Hunter Valley coal network and the interstate corridor, providing access to private operators.
Operations

items: Network Control Centres in Junee (NSW), Mile End (SA) and Broadmeadow (Hunter).
Inland Rail project — new Melbourne–Brisbane standard-gauge corridor.
Coordinates with NSW emergency services for level-crossing incidents and trespass.

overview.text: Blue Mountains City Council is the local government area covering the Blue Mountains region west of Sydney. Council services include parks and reserves, waste, roads, bushfire mitigation works and emergency operations support.
At a Glance

items: Population around 80,000 spread across 27 towns and villages.
World Heritage–listed Greater Blue Mountains Area covers most of the LGA.
Operates a 24/7 control centre during major incidents and runs an extensive volunteer bushfire-mitigation crew program alongside the RFS.

overview.text: Central Tablelands Water is a NSW county council that supplies bulk and reticulated drinking water to towns in Blayney, Cabonne and Weddin shires.
Coverage

items: Constituent councils: Blayney, Cabonne, Weddin.
Sources from Lake Rowlands and treatment plant near Carcoar.
Serves towns including Grenfell, Canowindra, Molong, Cudal, Eugowra and Manildra.

overview.text: Corrective Services NSW (CSNSW) is the executive agency responsible for managing the state's adult correctional system. It delivers professional correctional services and programs intended to reduce re-offending and enhance community safety, covering both custodial operations and the supervision of offenders in the community.

CSNSW operates as part of the Department of Communities and Justice. Its custodial arm manages remand and sentenced inmates across correctional centres statewide, while its Community Corrections arm supervises offenders serving orders in the community. It does not administer youth detention, which sits with Youth Justice NSW.
At a Glance

items: Manages 36 correctional centres across NSW, with 33 operated directly by CSNSW and 3 run by private providers
Employed about 10,610 staff as of 30 June 2025
Community Corrections supervises offenders on Intensive Correction Orders, Community Correction Orders, Conditional Release Orders and parole, including electronic monitoring
Part of the Department of Communities and Justice, under the Minister for Corrections
Traces its origins to 1788, with a dedicated Department of Prisons formed in 1874 and the Corrective Services name adopted in 1970
Operates the Corrective Services Academy at Brush Farm, Eastwood, a registered training organisation issuing nationally recognised qualifications from Certificate III to Advanced Diploma

overview.text: Structure

items: Organised into three main divisions: Strategy and Governance; Security and Custody; and Community, Industry and Capacity
Runs specialised facilities including the High Risk Management Correctional Centre, the Compulsory Drug Treatment Correctional Centre and Long Bay Hospital
Administers parole supervision in support of the State Parole Authority, alongside victim support and restorative justice services

overview.text: Endeavour Energy operates the electricity distribution network across Sydney's Greater West, the Blue Mountains, the Southern Highlands and the Illawarra — connecting around 2.7 million people and a million end-customers.
Coverage

items: Network area roughly 25,000 km².
Around 38,000 km of distribution lines.
24/7 control centre dispatches network-emergency crews and coordinates with NSW emergency services for downed-wire incidents.

overview.text: Fire and Rescue NSW (FRNSW) is the New South Wales State Government agency responsible for providing fire, rescue and hazardous materials (hazmat) services in the cities and towns of New South Wales. Formerly known as NSW Fire Brigades, it was renamed in 2011 to reflect its expanded rescue role, and is described as the busiest fire and rescue service in Australia and one of the largest urban fire services in the world.

Beyond structural and bush firefighting, FRNSW responds to road crash, industrial, confined space, vertical and other technical rescues, hazmat incidents, and maintains an accredited Urban Search and Rescue (USAR) task force. It operates 24 hours a day under the Fire and Rescue NSW Act 1989 and related emergency management legislation, with its stated purpose to protect life, property and the environment.
At a Glance

items: Established in 1910 as NSW Fire Brigades; tracing back to the Metropolitan Fire Brigade of 1884
Renamed Fire and Rescue NSW in 2011
Around 3,700 permanent (full-time) firefighters
Around 3,200 retained (on-call) firefighters
Around 550 trades and administrative staff, plus roughly 5,000 Community Fire Unit volunteers
335 fire stations across New South Wales
Headquartered at Greenacre, with communication centres at Alexandria and Newcastle and a training academy at Orchard Hills
A NSW State Government agency, led by a Commissioner

overview.text: Fire Officer Ranks

items: Recruit
Qualified Firefighter
Senior Firefighter
Leading Firefighter
Station Officer
Leading Station Officer
Inspector
Superintendent
Chief Superintendent
Assistant Commissioner
Deputy Commissioner
Commissioner

Final summary of actions taken and outcome.
Routine, non-incident traffic (station moves, returning to area, etc.).

Bateau Bay, Berkeley Vale, Budgewoi, Doyalson, Gosford, Hamlyn Terrace, Kariong, Ourimbah, Saratoga, Terrigal, The Entrance, Toukley, Umina, Wyoming, Wyong, Kincumber

Albion Park, Balgownie, Bulli, Coledale, Dapto, Helensburgh, Kiama, Shellharbour, Thirroul, Unanderra, Warrawong, Wollongong

Blackheath, Glenbrook, Katoomba, Lawson, Mount Victoria, Richmond, Springwood, Wallerawang, Wentworth Falls, Windsor

sections.intro: Common Fire and Rescue NSW appliance prefixes, grouped by type.

Tanker with Compressed Air Foam System

overview.text: Forestry Corporation of NSW manages around 2 million hectares of State Forests for timber production, recreation, fire management, biodiversity and Aboriginal cultural heritage. It maintains an internal fire-fighting capability and works closely with the RFS during the bushfire season.
Operations

items: Plantation and native-forest harvesting; timber sales for hardwood and softwood products.
Operational fire-management capability with crews, dozers, water-bombing aircraft contracts.
Recreation: 100+ campgrounds, 4WD trails, mountain-bike parks (e.g. Penrose, Cumberland, Sugar Pines).

overview.text: Hatzolah Medical Sydney is a volunteer-based emergency medical first-response service that primarily serves the Jewish community in Sydney's eastern suburbs. Staffed by trained volunteer emergency medical technicians and paramedics, it operates around the clock, every day of the year, providing rapid medical care in the critical minutes of an emergency.

Hatzolah complements NSW Ambulance rather than replacing it. Its volunteers act as first responders who reach and stabilise patients until an ambulance arrives. Since 2014 it has operated as an accredited NSW Ambulance Community First Responder unit, responding as a back-up to paramedics and to triple-zero (000) tasks allocated by NSW Ambulance when its volunteers are closest to the scene.
At a Glance

items: Volunteer-run emergency medical first-response service
Began responding to emergencies in 2006
Serves Sydney's eastern suburbs, including areas such as Bondi, Maroubra and Watsons Bay
Accredited as a NSW Ambulance Community First Responder unit since 2014
Coordinates with NSW Ambulance and responds to triple-zero (000) tasks
Available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, all year round
A charity funded entirely by community donations; the service is free to anyone who needs it

overview.text: Hunter Water provides drinking water, wastewater and recycled-water services to around 600,000 people across the Lower Hunter — Newcastle, Lake Macquarie, Maitland, Cessnock, Port Stephens and Dungog.
Operations

items: Around 250,000 connected properties.
Major sources: Chichester Dam, Grahamstown Dam, Tomago/Anna Bay sandbeds.
24/7 incident response for main breaks and sewage overflows.

overview.text: Marine Rescue NSW is the volunteer-based emergency service responsible for search and rescue on the waters of New South Wales. Its stated mission is to save lives on the water, operating 24 hours a day, every day, along the coastline and on designated inland waterways.

The organisation responds to marine emergencies, monitors marine radio for distress calls, and runs the state's only Log On and vessel tracking service for recreational boaters. It also delivers boating safety education and licensing, and provides operational support for events on Sydney Harbour and other waterways.
At a Glance

items: Established on 1 July 2009; began operating as a unified service on 1 January 2010
More than 3,200 volunteers
46 units across the NSW coastline, Lord Howe Island, the Alpine Lakes in the Snowy Mountains, and the Murray River at Moama
Fleet of about 100 craft: 80 rescue vessels and 20 Rescue Water Craft, plus 46 support vehicles
Organised into three zones covering six regions: Northern, Central and Southern
A Registered Training Organisation, training volunteers to national standards
In a reported year, launched 3,905 rescue missions and returned 8,918 boaters safely to shore

overview.text: History

items: Formed by merging three volunteer services
Royal Volunteer Coastal Patrol, established 1937
Australian Volunteer Coast Guard Association, established 1961
NSW Volunteer Rescue Association marine fleet, established 1969

overview.text: The Newborn and paediatric Emergency Transport Service (NETS) is a statewide service of NSW Health that provides specialist emergency medical retrieval and inter-hospital transport for critically ill or injured newborns, infants and children up to 16 years of age. Often described as "a hospital with no walls," it operates around the clock across New South Wales and the Australian Capital Territory, giving local clinicians access to intensive-care-level expertise regardless of location.

Beyond transport, NETS delivers expert clinical advice, clinical coordination and emergency stabilisation. Its Clinical Coordination Centre takes calls from clinicians every day and connects them with NETS retrieval specialists, arranging conference calls with senior experts before and during a transfer. NETS is part of the Sydney Children's Hospitals Network.
At a Glance

items: Established in 1977, consolidated into a single statewide service in 1995
Serves New South Wales and the Australian Capital Territory, supporting more than 250 hospitals
Operates a 24/7 Clinical Coordination Centre reached via 1300 362 500
Clinical team includes doctors (consultant retrievalists, registrars and career medical officers), specialist nurses, emergency vehicle operators and biomedical and support staff
Uses specially equipped road ambulances and dedicated helicopter transport (pioneered through the Child Flight service)
Gained legal ambulance service status in 2007; satellite services in Newcastle and Canberra formalised in 2008
Main base at Bankstown Airport in Sydney, relocated there in 2017
Governed as a statewide service of NSW Health within the Sydney Children's Hospitals Network

overview.text: Reach and Impact

items: Around one in every 49 children will travel with a NETS team at some point in childhood, rising to about one in 38 in rural areas
An average NETS mission takes over six hours
Only about 10 NSW hospitals provide neonatal intensive care and just three offer paediatric intensive care, making specialist transport essential

overview.text: NSW Ambulance is the statutory provider of pre-hospital emergency care, rescue and medical retrieval services across New South Wales. It operates as part of the NSW Health system, delivering mobile clinical care to people with emergency and medical needs throughout the state.

The service responds to emergency triple-zero calls, provides non-emergency patient transport, and undertakes aeromedical and specialist retrieval work. Around one in six people in NSW receive care from NSW Ambulance each year.
At a Glance

items: Ambulance services in NSW date to 1895, when the Civil Ambulance and Transport Brigade was established; the present agency was formally constituted in 2014.
Employs more than 6,100 staff, including 4,952 paramedics.
Operates from around 220 locations across the state, using more than 1,600 response vehicles.
Serves a population of roughly 7.9 million people across about 801,600 square kilometres.
Responds to approximately 1.1 million calls each year.
Operates a Medical Retrieval Unit with helicopter bases at Bankstown, Albion Park, Orange, Belmont, Tamworth and Lismore, staffed by doctors and critical care paramedics.
Supported by more than 350 clinical volunteers providing first-response care at around 60 locations.
Governed under the Health Services Act 1997 (NSW) as part of NSW Health.

overview.text: History

items: 1895: Civil Ambulance and Transport Brigade established.
1912: First motorised ambulance entered service.
1967: Air Ambulance Service established.
1991: All ambulances equipped with defibrillators.
2018: Paramedicine became a nationally regulated profession.

sections.intro: NSW Ambulance category codes and whether they usually run lights and sirens.

rows: Patient is not breathing, unconscious, or in a serious time-critical condition.
Patient is conscious and breathing but still serious and requires urgent response.
Moderate condition requiring ambulance response but not lights & sirens.

overview.text: The NSW Department of Primary Industries (DPI) was the state government agency responsible for agriculture, fisheries and aquaculture, forestry, and biosecurity. It worked to drive innovation and manage risks across the state's primary industries and natural resources, and it also carried significant regulatory and compliance responsibilities covering biosecurity, food safety, and animal welfare.

On 1 July 2024 DPI was abolished as a standalone department and its functions were absorbed into the renamed Department of Primary Industries and Regional Development (DPIRD), formed when the former Department of Regional NSW took on the primary industries portfolio. DPI's operations and branding continue as part of DPIRD, which reports to the Minister for Agriculture.
At a Glance

items: Core remit covered agriculture, fisheries and aquaculture, forestry, and biosecurity across New South Wales.
Key operating areas included DPI Agriculture, DPI Fisheries, and DPI Biosecurity and Food Safety.
Child agencies included the Forestry Corporation of NSW and the NSW Food Authority.
Drew regulatory powers from more than sixty acts, including the Biosecurity Act 2015, the Fisheries Management Act 1994, and the Forestry Act 1916.
Now operates within the Department of Primary Industries and Regional Development (DPIRD) following the 1 July 2024 merger.
Head office is located in the Ian Armstrong Building at 105 Prince Street, Orange.

overview.text: Regulatory and Compliance Functions

items: Fisheries compliance officers enforce fishing rules to conserve fish stocks and habitats, with powers including formal cautions, stop-work directions, and arrest where an offence is committed or an officer is threatened or assaulted.
Biosecurity and Food Safety manages risks from animal and plant pests, diseases, weeds, and contaminants, and oversees food production and labelling.
Authorised officers act under the Biosecurity Act 2015 and Fisheries Management Act 1994; inspectors also enforce the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act 1979 and Food Act 2003.
Prosecution is reserved for the most serious food safety, biosecurity, and animal welfare offences to achieve compliance and deterrence.

overview.text: The New South Wales Environment Protection Authority (EPA) is an independent statutory authority and the state's primary environmental regulator. It is constituted under the Protection of the Environment Administration Act 1991 and works to protect air, water, land and human health across NSW.

The EPA regulates pollution, waste, chemicals, pesticides, dangerous goods, contaminated land, radiation and native forestry. It sets and enforces environmental standards, licenses industry, and leads the response to serious pollution and environmental incidents, including operating the state's environmental incident reporting line.
At a Glance

items: Constituted under the Protection of the Environment Administration Act 1991; its principal operating law is the Protection of the Environment Operations Act 1997.
Re-established as an independent authority with its own governing board in February 2012.
Governed by a skills-based board and answerable to the NSW Minister for the Environment.
Regulates air, water and noise pollution, waste, chemicals, pesticides, dangerous goods, contaminated land, radiation and native forestry.
Leads response to serious environmental incidents and investigates major pollution events.
Employs roughly 500 staff, based in Sydney with regional offices across NSW.
Sits within the Department of Climate Change, Energy, the Environment and Water and reports annually on its performance.

overview.text: What It Regulates

items: Air quality, including industrial, motor vehicle and wood-smoke emissions
Waste, illegal dumping and litter
Industrial chemicals and contaminated land, including the site auditor scheme
Pesticides and dangerous goods transport
Radiation sources and user licensing

overview.text: NSW Mines Rescue is a service of Coal Services Pty Ltd (jointly owned by the NSW black-coal industry and the CFMEU). It provides specialist underground rescue capability, mine-emergency response and breathing-apparatus expertise to NSW coal mines and metalliferous operations.
Stations

items: Stations at Argenton (Hunter), Singleton, Lithgow, Woonona and Wollongong.
On-call brigade members are also working miners at member sites.
Capabilities: long-duration BA, irrespirable-atmosphere rescue, gas analysis, sealing teams, casualty extraction.

overview.text: The NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service manages more than 870 national parks, nature reserves and historic sites covering around 7.5 million hectares — about 9% of NSW. NPWS is a major fire-fighting agency and the lead manager for park-based emergencies and search-and-rescue.
Capabilities

items: Field staff trained as remote-area firefighters and as Park Rangers.
Hazard-reduction burning and fire suppression alongside RFS and Forestry Corporation.
Pest, weed and threatened-species management programs.
Coordinates with NSW Police Rescue and SES for park search and rescue.

overview.text: The New South Wales Police Force is the principal law enforcement agency of the Australian state of New South Wales. It is the largest police force in Australia and is responsible for policing across the entire state, with local government authorities holding only very limited enforcement roles.

The Force was established in 1862 under the Police Regulation Act 1862, which amalgamated the colony's separate police units into a single centrally controlled organisation. The amalgamation took effect on 1 March 1862 under Inspector General John McLerie.
At a Glance

items: Established in 1862 under the Police Regulation Act 1862
Around 17,348 sworn officers and 21,455 total employees (including support staff)
Jurisdiction covers about 801,600 square kilometres and a population exceeding 8.2 million
Operates 432 police stations organised into six regions
Led by Commissioner Mal Lanyon, sworn in on 1 October 2025 as the 24th Commissioner
Overseen by the New South Wales Minister for Police
Headquartered at 1 Charles Street, Parramatta

overview.text: Structure

items: Six regions divide the state's local policing
Metropolitan areas are covered by Police Area Commands (PACs)
Rural areas are covered by Police Districts (PDs)
Specialist commands support local police with additional operational services

overview.text: The NSW Rural Fire Service (NSW RFS) is a volunteer-based firefighting agency and statutory body of the New South Wales Government. It is the lead combat agency for bush and grass fires in NSW and provides fire and emergency services to roughly 95 percent of the state, covering areas outside the urban districts served by Fire and Rescue NSW.

Beyond bush and grass fires, the RFS responds to structural and vehicle fires, motor vehicle accidents, storms and floods, and assists other agencies. It is also the leading agency for bush fire management and mitigation, running hazard-reduction and risk-management programs across rural fire districts. It is widely described as the world's largest volunteer fire service.
At a Glance

items: Established 1 September 1997 under the Rural Fires Act 1997, succeeding the earlier Bush Fires Act arrangements
Around 69,624 volunteers, supported by about 1,333 paid staff
Nearly 2,000 rural fire brigades (about 1,999)
44 RFS districts, each operating a Fire Control Centre that coordinates local response
Serves roughly 110 local government areas across NSW
Covers approximately 95 percent of New South Wales
Attended more than 32,739 incidents across the state in 2024/25
Headquarters at Sydney Olympic Park

overview.text: Structure

items: The 44 districts are grouped into seven Area Commands: Hunter, Western, Greater Sydney, North Western, South Western, North Eastern and South Eastern, replacing the former four Regions
The Commissioner serves as the agency's Chief Executive Officer
Each district's Fire Control Centre coordinates local brigade responses to fires, natural disasters, motor vehicle accidents and other emergencies

sections.intro: RFS radio priority levels used when calling Fire Control.

overview.text: The New South Wales State Emergency Service (NSW SES) is a volunteer-based emergency and rescue organisation that serves communities across New South Wales before, during and after emergencies. It is the state's lead combat agency for floods, storms and tsunami, responsible for community preparedness, emergency warnings, response coordination and recovery for those hazards.

Beyond its lead-agency role, NSW SES carries out flood rescue, land search and rescue, road crash rescue and vertical rescue, and provides support to other agencies including the NSW Rural Fire Service during bushfires, NSW Ambulance, and the NSW Police Force for crash response and traffic management.
At a Glance

items: Founded in 1955; marked 70 years of service in 2025.
Operates as the combat agency for flood, storm and tsunami under the State Emergency Service Act 1989 (NSW).
More than 10,500 members, of whom about 97% are volunteers.
Over 260 volunteer units distributed across the state.
Organised into seven operational zones.
Part of the NSW Government, accountable to the Minister for Emergency Services.
Led by a Commissioner, with State Headquarters in Wollongong.

overview.text: Zones and Structure

items: Seven zones: Metro, Northern, North Eastern, North Western, Southern, South Eastern and Western.
Each zone is managed by a Zone Commander supported by a Zone Team.
Zones report through two Assistant Commissioners covering Metro & South and North & West operations.

overview.text: The NSW Telco Authority builds, maintains and operates the State's Public Safety Network (PSN) — the digital trunked radio network that consolidates the previous Government Radio Network (GRN) and serves the police, fire, ambulance, SES, health, transport, utilities and many councils.
Why You're Hearing Them

items: TGA owns the network — its own talkgroups are typically site-test, engineering and field-maintenance traffic.
Operates a 24/7 Network Operations Centre.
Migration of NSW agencies onto the PSN consolidates emergency communications statewide.

office-of-the-sheriff-of-nsw.title: ⚖ Office of the Sheriff of NSW

badges.label: NSW Department of Communities and Justice

overview.text: The Office of the Sheriff of New South Wales is a NSW Government agency within the Department of Communities and Justice (DCJ), headed by the State's Sheriff. It is Australia's oldest law enforcement agency, having operated continuously since 1824, and its Sheriff's Officers work to protect the NSW justice system and support the safe operation of the state's courts.

Its core role spans four functions: providing security at court complexes, administering the jury system, enforcing writs and property seizure orders on behalf of the courts, and supporting courtroom operations. Officers screen people entering court buildings, serve legal documents, execute warrants, and seize and sell property to satisfy court orders.
At a Glance

items: Established in 1824, making it Australia's oldest law enforcement agency, with over 200 years of continuous operation.
Part of the NSW Department of Communities and Justice (DCJ).
Staffed by more than 400 trained Sheriff's Officers, court officers, and clerical personnel.
Operates from 58 offices, servicing more than 180 court and tribunal locations across NSW.
Administers the NSW jury system, with more than 200,000 citizens engaging with the justice system through its operations each year.
Enforces writs, warrants, and court orders, including property seizure and public auction of seized assets.
Provides court security, including perimeter screening of people entering court complexes.

overview.text: Values and Initiatives

items: Guided by five core values: service, trust, accountability, integrity, and respect.
Committed to timely, people-centred service under its Service Charter.
Piloted body-worn cameras among Sheriff's Officers in 2023, evaluated in partnership with Charles Sturt University.

overview.text: Property NSW is the NSW Government's property-management entity. It manages a large portfolio of owned and leased commercial buildings, oversees major precincts (e.g. Darling Harbour, The Rocks, Sydney Olympic Park as Place Management NSW) and provides facilities-management services to other agencies.
Functions

items: Owned office portfolio in Sydney CBD and major regional centres.
Heritage and place-management at iconic precincts.
Security and operations talkgroups support 24/7 site management.

overview.text: Rental Groups are PSN talkgroups leased on a short or long-term basis to organisations that don't have their own permanent allocation. They cover event production, contractor crews, pop-up operations and one-off government projects.
Common Uses

items: Major-event security and traffic (e.g. Vivid Sydney, NYE, Mardi Gras, Sydney Royal Easter Show, marathon and triathlon support).
Construction tier-one contractors during major-project blockades.
Short-term agency surge capacity.

overview.text: Notes

items: Renter identity is rarely advertised in the PSN talkgroup name and frequently changes.
Encryption settings vary by hire customer.

overview.text: RSPCA NSW is a not-for-profit animal welfare charity operating across New South Wales, founded in 1873. It promotes the respectful and humane treatment of animals through animal care and rehoming, veterinary services, community education, and advocacy, supporting more than 180,000 animals each year.

Beyond its charitable work, RSPCA NSW holds a statutory law-enforcement role. Its inspectors are authorised to investigate and prosecute animal cruelty under the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act 1979 (POCTAA), which is why the organisation appears among enforcement agencies. RSPCA NSW inspectors were first recognised as special constables in 1943, giving them investigative and enforcement powers such as executing search warrants and seizing animals at risk.
At a Glance

items: Established in 1873; received its Royal Warrant in 1923
Around 40 inspectorate staff, led by a Chief Inspector, enforce the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act 1979
Supports more than 180,000 animals and roughly 200,000 people across NSW each year
Operates four shelters and adoption centres, two behaviour and rehabilitation centres, and three veterinary hospitals
Backed by about 549 employees, 2,094 volunteers, and 1,032 foster carers
Cruelty reports are received via the 1300 CRUELTY hotline
Independent charity funded largely by public donations, with only a small share from the NSW Government
Governed by an elected volunteer Board overseeing its strategic direction

overview.text: Shared Liaison Groups are talkgroups that bring multiple emergency services onto a single common channel for incident coordination. They are used at major incidents where two or more services need to talk to each other in real time without leaving the PSN.
Typical Patches

items: FRNSW + NSW Ambulance + NSW Police at structure fires and rescues.
RFS + FRNSW + Forestry + NPWS during major bushfires.
SES + NSW Ambulance + NSW Police during flood operations.
Multi-agency event management for major events (NYE, Vivid, Royal Show).

overview.text: A special constable is an auxiliary or specially appointed law enforcement officer granted defined police powers, typically limited to a particular jurisdiction, site, or piece of legislation rather than exercising the full general duties of a sworn police officer. Their powers, arming, and training vary widely depending on the appointing authority.

In New South Wales, the NSW Police Force employs Special Constables as an armed internal security force under its Security Management Branch. They provide protective security at key NSW Police and government premises, including NSW Parliament House, the Governor of NSW's residence, the courts and Director of Public Prosecutions, the Premier's Office, the Independent Commission Against Corruption, and covert police locations.
At a Glance

items: Appointed only by the NSW Police Commissioner under the Police Act 1990, which may confer on them the functions of a constable, including powers under the Law Enforcement (Powers and Responsibilities) Act 2002.
Armed with Glock pistols and standard NSW Police equipment, and hold powers of arrest.
Deployed to protect NSW Parliament House, the Governor's residence, courts, ICAC, the DPP, police headquarters, and other high-security sites.
Duties include being the first point of contact for visitors, monitoring electronic access points, and watching CCTV security footage.
Differ from general duties police in having a security-focused, site-based role rather than broad frontline policing.
Complete paid on-the-job training and typically work 12-hour rotational day or night shifts.
A Major Incident Security Team (MIST) provides rapid deployment with advanced firearms and defensive tactics training for special assignments.

overview.text: St John Ambulance NSW is the New South Wales arm of St John Ambulance Australia, a volunteer-based charity that provides first aid coverage at public events, delivers first aid and CPR training, and runs community care and youth programs. It is a member of the Order of St John, a humanitarian organisation whose history dates back more than 900 years.

It is not the state's emergency ambulance service; that role belongs to NSW Ambulance. Instead, St John Ambulance NSW complements the public system by acting as a medical reserve, staffing events with first aiders and volunteers, teaching the wider community to respond to a health crisis, and supporting responses to natural disasters and emergencies.
At a Glance

items: Member of the Order of St John, with roots tracing to an 11th-century hospice in Jerusalem; the modern St John Ambulance was established in 1877 and set up in Australia from 1883.
Operates with a base of thousands of volunteer members across New South Wales.
Provides first aid medical services at events year-round, including sports, music, cultural and major events such as the Sydney Royal Easter Show.
Delivers nationally recognised first aid, CPR and mental health training as a registered training organisation (RTO 88041) on behalf of St John Ambulance Australia.
Runs youth and cadet divisions through the St John Youth Program, with structured leadership progression for young members.
Governed as a charity by a board of non-executive directors and a volunteer Commissioner, led by a chief executive and executive team.
Headquartered at Macquarie Park in northern Sydney.

overview.text: Programs and Services

items: Community programs including First Aid in Schools, Driver First Aid education and the AED Point public defibrillator access initiative.
Workplace health services such as first aid kit restocking, defibrillator checks, site assessments and health staffing.
Deployment of volunteers as a medical reserve during natural disasters and emergencies, including the 2019-2020 bushfires and the COVID-19 response.

overview.text: Surf Life Saving NSW (SLSNSW) is the state's peak water safety and rescue organisation, a volunteer-based movement that protects people along the New South Wales coastline. Its members provide surf rescue and beach patrols, drowning prevention, first aid, and public education, working to achieve a vision of zero preventable deaths on the NSW coastline.

Beyond volunteer patrols, SLSNSW coordinates emergency responses and callouts through a State Operations Centre that runs 365 days a year, and it operates the Australian Lifeguard Service in NSW, which supplies paid lifeguards to councils and other locations. It also runs uncrewed aerial vehicle (drone) surveillance, event safety services, and a training academy.
At a Glance

items: The New South Wales Surf Bathing Association, its founding body, was formally established on 18 October 1907; the movement's origins trace to William Gocher at Manly Beach in 1902.
Around 75,000 members across NSW, including more than 20,000 active patrol members and over 30,000 junior 'Nippers' under age 14.
129 surf life saving clubs operating throughout New South Wales.
Organised into 10 regional branches spanning more than 1,500 kilometres of coastline.
Volunteers contribute in excess of 650,000 patrol hours each year, over a season running roughly September to April.
The Australian Lifeguard Service in NSW covers over 90 locations with seasonally more than 330 lifeguards.

overview.text: Branches

items: Far North Coast
North Coast
Mid North Coast
Lower North Coast
Hunter
Central Coast
Sydney Northern Beaches
Sydney
Illawarra
South Coast

overview.text: Sutherland Shire Council is the local government for southern Sydney, covering an area from Caringbah and Cronulla in the east through to Engadine, Menai and Royal National Park to the south and west.
At a Glance

items: Population around 230,000.
Operates lifeguards, parks, waste, environmental health, emergency-management coordination.
Talkgroups support council operations including ranger patrols, parks, libraries and waste services.

overview.text: The Sydney Opera House is one of Australia's most recognised landmarks and a UNESCO World Heritage site. The Opera House Trust manages venue operations, security, fire-safety, technical production and patron services across multiple performance halls.
Notes

items: Hosts more than 1,800 performances per year drawing 1.4M attendees.
Operates a 24/7 site security and operations team — talkgroups support venue, FOH, stage door, security and maintenance.
Coordinates with NSW Police and FRNSW for events and emergencies on-site.

overview.text: Sydney Water provides drinking water, wastewater, recycled water and some stormwater services to about 5.4 million people in Greater Sydney, the Illawarra and the Blue Mountains. Its network is the largest urban water network in Australia.
Network

items: Around 22,000 km of water mains and 26,000 km of sewer mains.
Treatment plants include Prospect (water) and several large coastal wastewater plants.
24/7 incident response for main breaks, sewage overflows and chemical spills.

overview.text: Transport for NSW is the lead State agency for the planning, regulation, integration and delivery of transport services and infrastructure across NSW. It oversees buses, ferries, light rail, trains, roads, maritime safety and active transport.
Structure

items: Established 2011, expanded in 2019 to absorb Roads & Maritime Services (RMS).
Operating divisions cover Sydney Trains, NSW TrainLink, Sydney Metro, Sydney Ferries, regional services and Transport Management Centres.
Operates the Transport Management Centre at Eveleigh — coordinates real-time response across road, rail, bus, ferry and light-rail.

overview.text: Sydney Ferries operates passenger ferry services across Sydney Harbour and the Parramatta River under contract to Transport for NSW. The fleet includes the iconic Manly Freshwater-class, the Emerald-class commuter ferries, RiverCats and Lady-class vessels.
Routes

items: F1 Manly · F2 Taronga Zoo · F3 Parramatta River · F4 Pyrmont Bay · F5 Neutral Bay · F6 Mosman Bay · F7 Eastern Suburbs · F8 Cockatoo Island · F9 Watsons Bay · F10 Blackwattle Bay.
Major wharves: Circular Quay, Barangaroo, Manly, Parramatta, Taronga Zoo.

overview.text: NSW Maritime is the regulator of recreational and commercial vessel safety on NSW waterways. It is part of Transport for NSW and works closely with the Marine Area Command (NSW Police), Marine Rescue NSW and AMSA.
Functions

items: Boating-safety regulation, licensing and registration.
Pollution response and aids-to-navigation maintenance.
Boating Safety Officers patrol popular waterways during peak periods.

badges.label: Sydney Trains / NSW TrainLink / Sydney Metro

overview.text: Heavy rail in NSW is delivered by three TfNSW operating entities: Sydney Trains (suburban), NSW TrainLink (intercity, regional and XPT services) and Sydney Metro (driverless metro). The network spans more than 1,500 km of track and 300+ stations.
Operators

items: Sydney Trains — eight suburban lines (T1–T9) plus the Airport line.
NSW TrainLink — Blue Mountains, Central Coast & Newcastle, South Coast, Southern Highlands, Hunter, plus regional XPT and Xplorer services.
Sydney Metro — Metro North West & City line; under expansion via Metro West and Western Sydney Airport line.
Rail Operations Centre coordinates response across the heavy-rail network with NSW Police Transit & Public Safety Command.

overview.text: TfNSW Roads (formerly Roads and Maritime Services) manages the State road network — motorways, highways, regional roads and major bridges and tunnels. It runs the Transport Management Centre, dispatches the Traffic Emergency Patrol, and oversees the road-asset network.
Capabilities

items: Transport Management Centre (TMC) — 24/7 incident coordination across road, rail, bus, ferry and light-rail.
Traffic Emergency Patrol — clears stalled vehicles and minor incidents to restore flow on motorways.
Heavy-vehicle and over-dimensional permit operations.

overview.text: Unconfirmed Groups are PSN talkgroups whose user agency has not been positively identified by the monitoring community, or where the prior allocation has changed. The TGIDs listed here may be active, dormant, reassigned, or temporarily allocated for testing.
Help Identify

items: If you have first-hand information about any of these talkgroups (especially their owning agency or function), please share via the project's data sources page so they can be re-categorised.
Verification typically requires distinct activity patterns, alias hints in audio, and corroboration with public-record information.
Never publish identifying information about private individuals heard on these channels.

overview.text: VRA Rescue NSW (Volunteer Rescue Association) is a volunteer-based emergency rescue organisation operating across New South Wales, providing rescue services 24 hours a day, every day of the year. It grew out of early bushwalker search-and-rescue efforts, with the first general land rescue squad forming in 1950 to assist during flood emergencies near Wagga Wagga.

Its volunteers deliver general land rescue, road crash rescue, vertical rescue, search operations, and storm and flood water rescue, concentrated across rural, regional, and remote parts of the state. Members are accredited by the Minister for Emergency Services, through the State Rescue Board, as rescue operators in their designated fields.
At a Glance

items: Formally established in 1969, when four squads (Albury, Dubbo, Narrandera, and Wagga Wagga) organised as the Volunteer Rescue Association
Around 45 volunteer squads operating from over 50 locations across New South Wales
Headquartered in Dubbo, NSW
Accredited rescue types include general land rescue, road crash rescue, vertical rescue, and flood water rescue
Additional capabilities include cave and alpine rescue, confined space rescue, search and rescue, drones, and communications support
Received government funding of $18.8 million over four years for new vehicles, equipment, and training
Coverage spans rural, regional, and remote NSW, including highways, forests, and alpine areas

overview.text: WaterNSW operates the State's bulk water-storage network — including Warragamba, the Snowy water transfers, the Hunter and the Murray-Darling regulated rivers. It supplies bulk water to Sydney Water and to regional councils, and licenses water for irrigation and town supply.
Assets

items: Operates more than 40 large dams including Warragamba, Burrendong, Wyangala, Copeton and Glenbawn.
Manages the State's water-licence and metering system.
Coordinates flood operations of regulated dams during major events.

overview.text: The Western Sydney Parklands Trust manages a 5,280-hectare urban parkland — one of the world's largest. It runs across Liverpool, Fairfield and Blacktown, providing recreation areas, cycleways, leasehold farms, and large bushland reserves.
At a Glance

items: Established 2006 by Act of Parliament.
Over 60 km of trails, picnic areas, dog parks and adventure playgrounds.
Ranger and operations talkgroups support 7-day-a-week site management.

overview.text: Youth Justice NSW is the New South Wales government agency responsible for supervising and caring for young people who have come into contact, or are at risk of coming into contact, with the criminal justice system. It supervises young people both in custody and in the community, administering court-imposed custodial and community-based orders as well as intervention programs aimed at preventing further offending.

It operates the state's youth justice centres and a network of community offices, and forms part of the Department of Communities and Justice. The agency was formerly known as the Department of Juvenile Justice.
At a Glance

items: Serves young people aged between 10 and 18 who have offended or are at risk of offending
Operates six youth justice centres across NSW: Cobham (Werrington), Frank Baxter (Kariong), Orana (Dubbo), Reiby (Airds), Riverina (Wagga Wagga) and Acmena (South Grafton)
Runs a statewide network of youth justice community offices supervising young people on community orders and delivering intervention programs
Provides individual case management, specialised counselling, and training in job and living skills
Part of the Department of Communities and Justice
Formerly the Department of Juvenile Justice (earlier the Office of Juvenile Justice)
